topic: Process - Spawning Processes with Custom Environment

turns.content: I'm stuck on my CI pipeline because the build server keeps getting weird environment variables injected into our Rust crates. I know how to use Command::env() in std, but why is it so different from just piping args like bash? Can you explain this?

You're right that the shell does something tricky with environment variables by default. When you call Command::current_dir() and then Command::envs(), the Rust std library gives you full control over what gets passed to the child process without relying on shell interpretation. The security implication here is huge—environment injection can happen if your env_clear() isn't used correctly, so you should always prefer explicit env(k,v) calls instead of blindly inheriting vars from the parent.

Wait, but I just tried that and it still failed. The logs show the build server is running with some default PATH variables that my Rust code isn't clearing out properly. Is there a way to completely strip all inherited vars like env_remove does?

You're right to be frustrated, but you need to call env_clear() first before setting your specific variables with Command::env(key, val). This ensures the child process starts with a clean slate rather than inheriting messy defaults from the build server. If you want to unset one particular variable like PATH, use env_remove instead of trying to clear everything manually.
